title: Lift at Central Park Tram Stop out of service, affecting access for commuters
excerpt: The lift at Central Park Tram Stop in Rochdale is out of service, impacting access for passengers travelling towards Oldham and raising concerns about public transport reliability.
content_html:
ONGOING STORY — Updated 9 July 2026 at 23:04. 4 source updates have been combined into this article.

The lift at Central Park Tram Stop in Rochdale is out of service, impacting access for passengers travelling towards Oldham and raising concerns about public transport reliability.

Latest update

The lift at Central Park Tram Stop in Rochdale has been out of service since 8:05 am on Thursday, 9 July 2026, as confirmed by the latest travel alerts from the Bee Network. This lift is essential for providing step-free access to the platform for passengers travelling towards Oldham, and its unavailability will remain in effect until further notice.

For those requiring step-free access, an alternative route has been established. Passengers can use the lift from The Gateway to reach the Manchester-bound platform, followed by a track crossing to access the Oldham-bound platform. This arrangement is particularly important for individuals with mobility challenges, as well as those travelling with prams or heavy luggage, who may find navigating the station without the lift difficult.

The disruption at Central Park Tram Stop is significant, given that it serves as a key interchange for many commuters and residents in the area. The absence of the lift is likely to lead to delays and inconvenience for those relying on public transport to reach work, school, or other essential appointments. This situation may disproportionately affect families, elderly passengers, and individuals with disabilities, who depend on accessible transport options.

Local authorities have expressed their apologies for the inconvenience caused and are actively working to resolve the issue as swiftly as possible. Passengers are encouraged to monitor updates on the Bee Network website or through live travel alerts to remain informed about the situation and any changes to service availability.

Earlier developments

This incident underscores the critical importance of accessible transport services in Rochdale. The malfunction of the lift not only disrupts daily commuting patterns but also raises broader concerns regarding the reliability of public transport infrastructure in the area. As the situation evolves, affected passengers may need to consider alternative routes and plan their journeys with potential delays in mind. Further updates will be provided as more information becomes available regarding the timeline for repairs.

Transport for Greater Manchester (TfGM) has implemented service changes on the Rochdale tram line due to planned works, which are currently in effect and will continue until further notice. This development is likely to disrupt the travel plans of many local commuters and residents who rely on this vital transport link.

As of Thursday, 9 July, at 9:54 PM, a tram fault has resulted in minor delays on the Eccles/Ashton service. Earlier that same day, at 8:18 PM, delays were also reported on the Airport line, attributed to an earlier road traffic collision. These ongoing disruptions compound the challenges faced by passengers during their daily commutes, potentially leading to longer wait times and altered travel schedules.

In addition to these immediate issues, TfGM has scheduled essential improvement works at the Prestwich Metrolink stop, set to take place from 18 May to 19 August. While tram services will not be directly affected, passengers may notice an increased presence of staff at this location during the improvement period, which could assist in managing the flow of commuters and providing information.

The cumulative effect of these service changes and delays may lead to increased congestion on alternative travel routes as commuters seek other means of transport. Families and individuals who depend on public transport for work or other commitments should be prepared for potential delays and consider allowing extra time for their journeys. It may also be prudent to explore alternative routes or modes of transport during this period.

Passengers are encouraged to check the TfGM travel alerts page for the latest updates and to remain informed about travel conditions. For those directly affected by these changes, TfGM offers assistance and clarification regarding travel plans, ensuring that commuters can navigate these disruptions as smoothly as possible. Further information can be accessed through the TfGM Travel Alerts page.

title: Rochdale Weather Forecast: Warm and Sunny Days Ahead
excerpt: Rochdale is set to experience a warm and sunny week, with temperatures peaking at 31°C on Friday. Residents are advised to prepare for high pollen levels and minimal rain.
content_html:
Rochdale is gearing up for a warm and sunny week, with the Met Office forecasting maximum temperatures reaching 31°C on Friday, July 10. The pleasant weather is expected to continue throughout the weekend, making it an ideal time for outdoor activities and events in the area.

Today, temperatures are expected to reach a high of 22°C, with clear skies and a light breeze. As the week progresses, the heat will intensify, with Friday marking the hottest day, where it will feel like 32°C due to humidity. Residents should be aware of the very high pollen levels, which could affect those with allergies.

Saturday will see a slight dip in temperatures, with a maximum of 28°C, but it will remain sunny. Sunday will continue the trend with highs of 26°C, providing a perfect opportunity for families to enjoy local parks and recreational areas. The forecast indicates minimal chances of rain throughout the week, with less than 10% expected on most days.

As the week unfolds, temperatures will remain steady, with Monday and Tuesday both expected to reach 26°C. The evenings will cool down to around 14-17°C, making for comfortable nights. Residents are encouraged to stay hydrated and protect themselves from the sun during peak hours, especially on the hotter days.

This warm weather is significant for local businesses, particularly those in the hospitality and outdoor leisure sectors, as it may boost foot traffic and sales. Parks and outdoor venues are likely to see increased visitors, which can positively impact the local economy.

For those planning outdoor activities, it is advisable to check the weather regularly and prepare for high pollen counts. The Met Office provides updated forecasts, which can be accessed online for the latest information. Residents can also report any weather-related issues or concerns through local council channels.

title: Bleakholt Animal Sanctuary extends partnership with The Rock Shopping Centre
excerpt: Bleakholt Animal Sanctuary has been selected as The Rock Shopping Centre’s charity partner for a second consecutive year, following a public vote. The partnership aims to raise funds and awareness for the sanctuary until June 2027.
content_html:
Bleakholt Animal Sanctuary has been reaffirmed as The Rock Shopping Centre’s charity partner for a second consecutive year, following a successful public vote. This renewed collaboration is set to continue until June 2027, with the primary aim of raising funds and increasing awareness for the sanctuary through a series of events and campaigns.

The Rock Shopping Centre, located in Bury, has confirmed that this partnership follows a productive first year, during which over £4,500 was raised for Bleakholt through various fundraising activities and awareness initiatives. Laura Beal, the Centre Manager, expressed her enthusiasm for the ongoing collaboration, noting the strong community support for the sanctuary's essential work.

Established over 60 years ago, Bleakholt Animal Sanctuary has been dedicated to providing care, shelter, and rehoming services for hundreds of animals. In addition to its core mission, the sanctuary engages in educational and community outreach, further solidifying its role within the local area. Karen Weed, the Sanctuary Manager, conveyed her gratitude for the continued partnership, emphasising the significant impact that the funds raised have on the lives of the animals in their care.

The renewed partnership will feature a variety of fundraising events and awareness campaigns designed to support the sanctuary's mission and highlight its contributions to the local community. The first year of collaboration included numerous events that not only generated financial support but also heightened public awareness regarding animal welfare and the services provided by Bleakholt.

This partnership is likely to enhance community spirit and encourage local residents to support a charity that plays a vital role in animal welfare. The commitment from The Rock Shopping Centre underscores the importance of local businesses in championing charitable causes and fostering community engagement. Residents interested in supporting Bleakholt Animal Sanctuary can participate in upcoming events at The Rock Shopping Centre, which will be announced throughout the year. For further information on how to get involved or to make a donation, individuals can visit Bleakholt's official website or contact The Rock directly.

title: Castleton Library to host Bookstart events for young children
excerpt: On Monday, 13 July, Castleton Library will host two Bookstart events aimed at promoting early literacy among young children, providing opportunities for parents to engage with their infants and toddlers.
content_html:
Castleton Library will host two events on Monday, 13 July, aimed at fostering early literacy among young children. The first, the Bookstart Bear Club, is scheduled from 9:45 AM to 10:30 AM, followed by the Bookstart Baby session from 11 AM to 11:30 AM. Both events are designed to cultivate a love for reading in infants and toddlers, encouraging engagement between parents and their children.

The Bookstart Bear Club caters to children aged 12 months and older, offering an interactive environment filled with stories, songs, and rhymes. This session aims to encourage parents and caregivers to actively participate in reading activities, thereby instilling a positive attitude towards books from an early age. Following this, the Bookstart Baby event is tailored for infants up to 12 months old, providing a nurturing space for parents to bond with their babies while introducing them to the joys of literature through stories and songs.

Both events are free of charge, but advance booking is essential due to limited capacity. These initiatives are part of the national Bookstart programme, which seeks to provide free books to children and promote reading as a crucial component of early childhood development. The sessions at Castleton Library not only support literacy but also create opportunities for parents to connect with other families in the community, fostering a sense of belonging and shared experience.

For local families, participation in these events may enhance their children's early learning experiences significantly. Engaging with literature at a young age is linked to improved language skills and cognitive development, which could have lasting benefits as children progress through their education. Moreover, the social aspect of these sessions may help parents build networks of support within the community, which can be invaluable as they navigate the challenges of early parenthood.

Interested attendees can secure their places by booking tickets through Eventbrite, where additional details about the events are also available. As the date approaches, parents are encouraged to arrive a few minutes early to ensure a smooth start to the sessions. For further inquiries, Castleton Library can be contacted directly.

title: Murder investigation launched following fatal fire at Rochdale farm
excerpt: Pakistan has declined to accept the deportation of Shabir Ahmed, the ringleader of a Rochdale grooming gang, following his release from prison. The UK government is now considering changes to immigration laws to facilitate his removal.
content_html:
ONGOING STORY — Updated 9 July 2026 at 12:22. 20 source updates have been combined into this article.

Pakistan has declined to accept the deportation of Shabir Ahmed, the ringleader of a Rochdale grooming gang, following his release from prison. The UK government is now considering changes to immigration laws to facilitate his removal.

Latest update

In a significant diplomatic development, Pakistan has formally refused to accept the deportation of Shabir Ahmed, the leader of a Rochdale grooming gang, who was released from prison last week after serving 14 years for multiple sexual offences against minors. This refusal has raised serious concerns among victims and local communities regarding their safety and the government's capacity to manage serious offenders.

Shabir Ahmed, now 73, was convicted in 2012 for his involvement in a grooming gang that exploited vulnerable girls, some as young as 12. Following his release on July 2, the Home Office has faced mounting pressure to amend existing immigration laws to facilitate his deportation. Ahmed, who held dual British-Pakistani citizenship, was stripped of his British nationality upon conviction, leaving him solely with Pakistani citizenship.

The UK government is currently contemplating changes to the 1971 Immigration Act, which restricts the removal of certain Commonwealth citizens who arrived in the UK prior to 1973. Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood has indicated that she will propose amendments to this legislation, which could take up to a year to implement. Such changes are deemed essential to enable the deportation of Ahmed, who has been assessed by parole officers as posing a 'very high risk of serious harm to children'.

The situation has escalated into a diplomatic dispute, with reports suggesting that Pakistan is demanding the extradition of two political dissidents in exchange for accepting Ahmed back. This complicates an already sensitive issue, as the UK government must balance international relations with pressing domestic safety concerns.

Earlier developments

Local MPs and community leaders have expressed outrage over the situation, articulating fears for the safety of victims and the broader community. Calls for urgent legislative action have intensified, with some politicians arguing that current laws are outdated and fail to adequately address the severity of Ahmed's crimes. As the government faces increasing pressure to act decisively, parliamentary debates on the proposed changes to immigration law are set to take place, with the next session scheduled for July 13. The outcome of these discussions will be closely monitored by local residents and victims, who are encouraged to engage with community forums and local representatives to voice their concerns.

A police officer was taken to hospital following a collision involving a Greater Manchester Police van in Middleton on Friday, July 3. The incident occurred at approximately 1.10pm on Townley Street, where the police van collided with a car. Fortunately, the officer's injuries are not believed to be life-threatening or life-changing, and he is currently receiving assessment and treatment at the hospital. No other injuries were reported as a result of the crash, and no arrests have been made in connection with the incident.

This collision is part of a concerning trend, as it was one of two similar incidents involving police vehicles on the same day across Greater Manchester. Later that evening, at around 8.45pm, a British Transport Police van was involved in a separate crash with a blue Kia Picanto on Rochdale Road in Harpurhey. At the time of this incident, officers were transporting a 28-year-old man to police custody. Thankfully, no serious injuries were reported in this case either.

The incidents raise questions about the safety of police operations on the roads, particularly when transporting individuals in custody. The Greater Manchester Police have not released further details regarding the circumstances leading up to the Middleton crash, but it is likely that an internal review will be conducted as part of standard procedure following such incidents. This review may examine the protocols in place for police vehicle operations, especially in high-pressure situations.

Local residents may have heightened concerns regarding road safety and the operational protocols of police vehicles in light of these accidents. The implications for the community could be significant, particularly for families and commuters who rely on Townley Street and surrounding roads. If the review identifies areas for improvement, it may lead to changes in how police vehicles operate, potentially enhancing safety for all road users.

As investigations continue, the police force is expected to provide updates to ensure transparency and accountability. The community is encouraged to report any suspicious activity to help maintain safety in the area. Anyone with information about either incident is urged to contact Greater Manchester Police via their website or by calling 101. In emergencies, the public should always dial 999.

Update timeline

title: Temporary fire station in Heywood approved during redevelopment
excerpt: Greater Manchester Fire and Rescue Service has secured approval for a temporary fire station in Heywood, ensuring continuous emergency services while the existing facility undergoes significant redevelopment.
content_html:
ONGOING STORY — This report brings together confirmed developments from multiple sources.

Greater Manchester Fire and Rescue Service (GMFRS) has received approval to establish a temporary fire station in Heywood, facilitating the redevelopment of the existing facility. The new structure will be situated behind the current station on Middleton Road, ensuring that emergency response services remain operational throughout the rebuilding process.

The existing fire station, which has served the Heywood community for nearly 60 years, is scheduled for demolition as part of this redevelopment initiative. The temporary station will consist of a two-storey modular building, equipped with a bay for one fire engine, while an additional outbuilding will house any specialist vehicles. This arrangement is intended to guarantee that emergency services continue without interruption during the construction period.

Planning documents reveal that the temporary fire station is strategically located just 400 metres from Junction 19 of the M62 motorway, positioning it well for rapid emergency response. GMFRS has stated that the new facility will be a modern, fit-for-purpose community fire station, designed to meet both current and future operational needs. It will also enhance facilities for staff training and community engagement, reflecting the service's commitment to adapting to the evolving demands of the community.

This redevelopment is part of a wider £38 million Estates Strategy by GMFRS, aimed at upgrading and modernising several fire stations across the Greater Manchester area. The strategy underscores the importance of ensuring that fire services can provide optimal support to residents, adapting to changing community needs and operational requirements. A separate planning application for the demolition of the existing station and the construction of the new community fire station is expected to be submitted shortly.

The anticipated new facility will include a training tower, vehicle parking, and a dedicated training yard, which will further enhance GMFRS's capabilities in the region. For local families, businesses, and schools, the uninterrupted provision of emergency services during the redevelopment is crucial, as it ensures that safety and response times remain unaffected. title: Health experts issue guidance on safe fan use amid Rochdale heatwave
excerpt: As temperatures rise, health experts in Rochdale provide essential advice on the safe operation of electric fans to mitigate overheating and electrical hazards.
content_html:
ONGOING STORY — This report brings together confirmed developments from multiple sources.

As the UK endures a significant heatwave, health and safety experts are advising Rochdale residents on the safe operation of electric fans. This guidance, issued by High Speed Training, underscores the importance of taking precautions to prevent overheating and potential electrical hazards as households increasingly rely on fans for relief from the soaring temperatures.

Dr Richard Anderson, Director of Learning at High Speed Training, emphasised the effectiveness of fans in providing comfort during hot weather while also stressing the necessity of their proper use to ensure safety. He noted that as temperatures rise, many individuals will be reaching for their fans, but adherence to safety protocols is crucial to avoid accidents.

Residents should register new fans with their manufacturers to receive important updates regarding safety recalls. Before using a fan, it is vital to inspect it for any signs of damage, particularly focusing on the plug and cable. Fans that produce buzzing or crackling noises, or exhibit warm plugs, should be turned off immediately and examined by a qualified electrician. Regular cleaning is also important; dust accumulation on fan blades can strain the motor and increase the risk of overheating. Users must always unplug the fan before cleaning and follow the manufacturer's reassembly instructions.

Furthermore, it is advisable to avoid leaving fans running unattended or overnight while sleeping. Instead, residents might consider cooling their bedrooms prior to bedtime by operating the fan with windows open when appropriate. Proper positioning of fans is equally important; they should be placed on a flat, stable surface and kept clear of curtains or other materials that could obstruct airflow or create hazards.

Dr Anderson reiterated the significance of these safety measures, asserting that dedicating a few moments to simple safety checks can prevent fires or accidents and ensure fans operate smoothly. With the heatwave projected to persist for at least another week, adhering to these safety tips will be crucial for maintaining comfort and safety during this period. For further information or to report safety concerns, residents can contact local health and safety authorities.

Conversely, some residents have expressed apprehension regarding the scheme's effectiveness. Critics suggest that the alterations may lead to increased congestion and longer travel times, particularly during peak hours. There are fears that the new traffic patterns could confuse drivers who are unfamiliar with the area, potentially negating the intended safety benefits.

The council has committed to closely monitoring the trial, with plans to gather feedback from residents to evaluate the scheme's impact. A review is scheduled to take place at the end of the trial period, which will determine whether the changes will be made permanent or adjusted based on community input. Local residents can submit their views through the council's official channels, ensuring that all perspectives are considered in this important safety initiative.

The implications of this trial scheme extend beyond mere traffic management. Families, particularly those with children, may find the changes reassuring if they lead to improved safety. title: Proposal for new SEND school at Kingsway West Business Park
excerpt: Whitehall Academy has submitted plans to convert a vacant warehouse into a school for children with special educational needs and disabilities, accommodating up to 50 pupils.
content_html:
ONGOING STORY — This report brings together confirmed developments from multiple sources.

A proposal has been submitted to transform a vacant warehouse at Kingsway West Business Park in Rochdale into a new educational facility for children with special educational needs and disabilities (SEND). The plans, put forward by Whitehall Academy, aim to create a school that will accommodate up to 50 pupils, providing essential support for children with complex needs.

The design of the proposed school includes six classrooms, three sensory rooms, and a multi-purpose hall, alongside various support spaces such as offices and a medical room. While the exterior of the building will remain unchanged, a secure play area featuring artificial grass is planned for the west side, utilising previously unused space. This initiative represents an extension of Whitehall Academy's existing operations on Whitehall Street, where the independent primary school already focuses on supporting vulnerable children with diverse needs.

Students at the proposed facility may include those diagnosed with autism and learning difficulties, all of whom will have an Education, Health, and Care Plan (EHCP) or be undergoing the assessment process. The school is expected to operate from 9am to 3pm for pupils, with staff hours extending from 8am to 4pm. There are also plans for breakfast and after-school clubs, which could extend the school day until 5pm, providing additional support for families.

The warehouse, previously occupied by Circuitmaster Designs Ltd, an engineering firm, has been vacant since October 2024. The conversion of this space into a SEND school is likely to address a pressing need for specialised educational facilities in the area, particularly as the demand for such services continues to grow.

The establishment of a SEND school at Kingsway West Business Park could have significant implications for local families and the wider community. Parents of children with special educational needs may find increased access to tailored educational resources and support, reducing the need for travel to distant facilities. Additionally, the school could foster a more inclusive environment within the neighbourhood, promoting awareness and understanding of SEND issues. As the proposal progresses, local stakeholders, including parents and community organisations, may wish to engage with the planning process to ensure that the needs of all affected parties are considered.

title: Thomas O’Rourke sentenced to 26 years for decades of child abuse
excerpt: Thomas O’Rourke has been sentenced to 26 years in prison after being convicted of multiple sexual offences against five children over a span of more than twenty years. The case highlights the importance of reporting abuse, regardless of when it occurred.
content_html:
Thomas O’Rourke, a 74-year-old resident of Rochdale, has been sentenced to 26 years in prison following his conviction for 31 sexual offences against five children. The sentencing took place at Manchester Crown Court on 6 July 2026, after a trial that revealed the extensive nature of his crimes, which occurred between the 1970s and 1990s in areas including Middleton and Blackley.

The charges against O’Rourke included serious offences such as rape, sexual assault, and indecent assault. During the trial, survivors delivered poignant victim impact statements, articulating the enduring effects of the abuse they suffered. One survivor spoke of her resolve to reclaim her voice and confront the pain inflicted by O’Rourke's actions. Another survivor expressed her struggle with the label of victim, hoping that O’Rourke would come to feel remorse for the suffering he caused. A third survivor reflected on the disruption to her childhood and teenage years, acknowledging that while it took time for her to report the abuse, her journey towards recovery would be even more protracted.

The investigation into O’Rourke’s actions commenced in 2019 when the first survivor bravely came forward, prompting detectives to uncover additional victims. Detective Inspector Wilcock, who led the investigation, praised the survivors for their courage and commitment to seeking justice, underscoring the police's determination to pursue cases of abuse irrespective of when they occurred. O’Rourke was characterised as a predator who exploited the trust of children, and his sentence serves as a powerful reminder that survivors can seek justice at any point in their lives.

This case has significant implications for the local community, particularly for families and educational institutions. The revelations surrounding O’Rourke's actions may encourage other survivors to come forward, fostering a culture of openness and support. Schools and community organisations may need to enhance their safeguarding measures and provide additional resources to support children and families affected by abuse. The case also highlights the importance of ongoing education about consent and healthy relationships, which could help prevent future incidents.

As the community processes the impact of this case, it is crucial for local support services to remain proactive and accessible. The police and various organisations are likely to continue their outreach efforts to ensure that survivors feel empowered to report abuse and receive the necessary support. The message from this case is clear: justice can be pursued at any time, and there is a network of support available for those who have suffered.

title: Heywood residents endure foul odours and drainage problems
excerpt: Residents of a Heywood apartment block have faced unsanitary conditions due to drainage issues linked to improper waste disposal, prompting calls for better management from their housing association.
content_html:
Residents of a newly developed apartment block on Rochdale Road East in Heywood have been grappling with persistent foul odours and unsanitary conditions stemming from drainage issues that have persisted for several days. The problems, first reported on Friday, July 3, have been linked to residents flushing inappropriate items, such as nappies and baby wipes, down the toilets, resulting in significant blockages within the drainage system.

The situation escalated dramatically when residents began to notice leaks in the underground car park and communal bin shed, with reports of faeces on the floors and a stench described as reminiscent of 'rotten eggs'. One resident, who preferred to remain anonymous, recounted the distressing conditions, stating that the overpowering smell had caused nausea among several residents, including a pregnant woman. Despite numerous calls to First Choice Homes Oldham, the housing association responsible for the building, residents felt their concerns were not being adequately addressed, expressing frustration at what they perceived as dismissive responses from staff.

"We feel like we are being ignored," said one resident, reflecting the sentiments of many in the building, which accommodates 40 apartments. In response to the complaints, First Choice Homes Oldham acknowledged the issue, confirming that the blockage was cleared on Monday, July 7, after being assessed over the preceding weekend. A spokesperson for the housing association indicated that the problem had been exacerbated by improper waste disposal practices among residents, and they intend to remind tenants of the importance of not flushing inappropriate items down the toilets.

The residents' grievances have raised broader concerns regarding the management of the property, particularly as the building remains within its defects period. While the housing association confirmed that necessary repairs were conducted by contractors, residents are left questioning the effectiveness of the response and the ongoing management of their living conditions. This incident underscores the critical importance of proper waste disposal and the potential ramifications of neglecting such practices.

As the area prepares for a second heatwave, residents are increasingly anxious about the implications of unsanitary conditions in their living environment. The combination of heat and poor hygiene could exacerbate health risks, particularly for vulnerable individuals. Many residents have expressed a desire for more proactive communication and support from First Choice Homes to prevent similar issues from arising in the future. The situation serves as a reminder of the need for effective property management and community cooperation to ensure a safe and healthy living environment.

title: Trial begins for man accused of murdering teenager in Middleton
excerpt: Terrence King is on trial for the alleged murder of 15-year-old Devon Simmonds-Caines, who was struck by King's car after stealing an e-bike.
content_html:
A trial has commenced at Manchester's Minshull Street Crown Court for Terrence King, who faces allegations of murdering 15-year-old Devon Simmonds-Caines in Middleton last year. The incident occurred on 22 July 2025, when Simmonds-Caines was struck by King's vehicle after allegedly stealing an e-bike from another boy.

The prosecution, led by Michael Hayton KC, contends that King, 60, deliberately drove his car at Simmonds-Caines as an act of revenge. CCTV and doorbell camera footage presented in court reportedly shows King accelerating before veering onto the pavement and colliding with the teenager, who succumbed to his injuries at the scene.

King has admitted to manslaughter, acknowledging that his actions resulted in Simmonds-Caines's death, but he denies the charge of murder. The prosecution argues that this incident was not merely a tragic accident or a case of poor driving, but rather a calculated act of aggression against a boy who had stolen the e-bike.

The footage shown in court was described as distressing, prompting some family members of Simmonds-Caines to leave the courtroom in tears. This case has attracted significant attention, particularly due to the victim's age and the circumstances surrounding his death, raising broader concerns about youth crime and vigilantism.

The community in Middleton is now grappling with the implications of such incidents, which raise pressing questions about safety and the appropriate responses to crime. The trial is expected to continue over the coming weeks, with further evidence and testimonies to be presented. Local residents, particularly those affected by the incident, may be closely following the proceedings as they seek to comprehend the full context of this tragic event and its impact on community safety.

title: Rochdale launches initiative to promote walking and cycling
excerpt: The Active Travel initiative encourages Rochdale residents to choose walking and cycling for short journeys, aiming to improve health and reduce traffic congestion in the borough.
content_html:
Rochdale has officially launched its Active Travel initiative, designed to encourage residents to opt for walking, wheeling, and cycling for short journeys. This programme is part of a wider strategy across Greater Manchester to promote healthier lifestyles, improve air quality, and alleviate traffic congestion within the borough. Delivered by Your Trust Rochdale in collaboration with Rochdale Borough Council, Transport for Greater Manchester, and Endurance Hub, the initiative seeks to make active travel the preferred choice for local trips, potentially reducing the number of car journeys made in the area.

The initiative's objectives highlight that millions of journeys in Greater Manchester are currently undertaken by car, many of which could be easily completed on foot or by bicycle. By increasing the number of residents who choose active travel options, the programme aims to enhance community health and wellbeing while contributing to environmental sustainability. Various activities and resources will be made available throughout the borough to support walking, wheeling, and cycling, fostering a culture of active travel within the community.

The focus of the initiative extends beyond individual health benefits; it also aims to create a more vibrant and accessible environment for all residents. By promoting active travel, the initiative could lead to a reduction in traffic congestion, which may improve journey times for those who still rely on motor vehicles. Families, particularly those with children, may find it easier to navigate local streets safely, while businesses could benefit from increased foot traffic as more residents choose to walk or cycle to local shops and services.

For those interested in learning more about the Active Travel initiative or participating in upcoming activities, the Active Travel team can be contacted via email at activetravel@yourtrustrochdale.co.uk or by calling 01706 922437. This outreach aims to engage the community and gather feedback on how to improve active travel options in Rochdale, ensuring that the initiative meets the needs of local residents.

title: Metrolink services in Rochdale disrupted by £150m upgrade works
excerpt: Three Metrolink lines will be closed for three weeks from 13 July as part of a £150 million upgrade, impacting local transport and commuter journeys.
content_html:
Transport for Greater Manchester (TfGM) has announced that three Metrolink lines will be closed for three weeks, commencing 13 July, due to essential upgrade works. This initiative is part of a broader £150 million investment aimed at enhancing the tram network across Greater Manchester. The lines affected by this closure include the Eccles, Trafford Centre, and Altrincham routes, which will not operate during this period. Furthermore, the East Didsbury and Manchester Airport lines will terminate at Firswood, while services on the Rochdale line will conclude at Exchange Square. The disruption is anticipated to last until 2 August.

Ian Davies, the network director for Metrolink, emphasised that the work is being carried out on one of the busiest stretches of tram track in the country, specifically between Trafford Bar, Cornbrook, and Deansgate-Castlefield. In addition to the tram line closures, further works will take place at Eccles and around Piccadilly Gardens in the city centre. This comprehensive approach is intended to minimise future disruptions to the network, ultimately ensuring a more reliable service once the upgrades are completed.

The impact of these closures is likely to be felt by commuters, families, and local businesses. Those relying on the affected tram services may need to adjust their travel plans, potentially leading to longer journey times as they switch to replacement bus services. TfGM has stated that these bus services will be available between the affected stops, but passengers should anticipate delays and plan accordingly. The timing of the upgrade works coincides with the special event bus service for The Hundred cricket matches at Old Trafford on 26 and 31 July, which could further complicate travel arrangements for those attending the matches or commuting in the area.

TfGM encourages passengers to remain informed about service changes and to check their travel options in advance. Updates will be provided through TfGM's official channels, ensuring that commuters have access to the latest information regarding tram services and replacement bus routes. This proactive communication is essential for minimising inconvenience during the upgrade period, as many local residents and commuters will be affected by the changes.

As the upgrade progresses, the long-term benefits of improved tram services are expected to enhance connectivity and reliability for the Rochdale area. While the immediate disruptions may pose challenges, the investment in infrastructure is likely to yield a more efficient transport network in the future, ultimately benefiting the local community.

title: Joseph Holt launches £1 children's meals and £20,000 charity appeal in Rochdale
excerpt: Joseph Holt brewery has unveiled a £1 children's meal deal and a £20,000 fundraising campaign to support local children during the summer holidays, addressing food insecurity in the community.
content_html:
Joseph Holt, the independent family-owned brewery, has introduced a £1 children's meal initiative across its pubs, including those in Rochdale. This campaign commenced on 25 June and will continue until 1 September, coinciding with the Government's temporary five per cent VAT reduction on children's meals served in pubs and restaurants. The price reduction represents a significant decrease of over 60 per cent from the usual cost of £5.45 for a children's meal, thereby making dining out more accessible for families during the school holidays.

In addition to the meal deal, families can opt to upgrade their children's meals to include a drink and an ice lolly for an additional £1.95. This initiative is particularly timely, as many families are currently facing increased financial pressures, and the affordability of meals could significantly ease the burden during the summer months when children are not in school.

Alongside the meal offer, Joseph Holt has initiated a fundraising campaign with the ambitious goal of raising £20,000 for the children's charity Meals & More. This charity is dedicated to combating holiday hunger by providing free nutritious meals and activities for children when free school meals are unavailable during the holidays. Jane Kershaw, a director at Joseph Holt and a sixth-generation member of the founding family, underscored the importance of community support, stating that the initiative aims to alleviate the distress of children going hungry during the summer break.

Kerri Frost, corporate partnerships manager at Meals & More, expressed gratitude for the partnership, noting the alignment of their mission with Joseph Holt's commitment to local communities. The funds raised will directly support children experiencing food insecurity, ensuring they have access to food and safe spaces during the holidays. The campaign will feature various fundraising activities across Joseph Holt's pubs, including raffles and bingo games, with all proceeds directed towards the charity.

This collaborative effort is likely to create a positive impact on the lives of children and families in Rochdale and the wider North West region. title: Trainspotting production returns to Ramsbottom amid record ticket sales
excerpt: The acclaimed production of Trainspotting will run at the Geoffrey Sword Studio from 12 to 18 July, following unprecedented demand that saw many performances sell out within hours.
content_html:
An award-winning production of Trainspotting will return to the Geoffrey Sword Studio in Ramsbottom from 12 to 18 July, following an extraordinary surge in ticket demand that resulted in several performances selling out within hours of the announcement. This revival is set to feature the original cast, who will once again depict the stark realities of addiction and friendship against the backdrop of 1980s Manchester.

The theatre reported its highest single-day ticket sales to date after confirming the show's return, reflecting a robust local interest in this immersive theatrical experience. Audiences have already begun booking for multiple performances, with the matinee on 18 July currently offering the best availability, while many evening shows are fully booked. Adapted by Harry Gibson and Jon Walker from Irvine Welsh's iconic novel, this production delves into the lives of Mark Renton and his friends as they confront the challenges of heroin addiction and the repercussions of their choices.

The immersive staging and direct audience engagement are designed to enhance the storytelling, providing a unique experience for theatre-goers. The cast includes well-known characters such as Sick Boy, Alison, and Tommy, alongside Renton, exploring themes of addiction, redemption, and the impact of substance misuse. The production has garnered accolades, including the title of Amateur Production of the Year from Stagey Reviews, underscoring its quality and relevance.

However, potential attendees should be mindful of the content warnings issued by the theatre. The production contains scenes of heavy drug use, strong language, nudity, violence, and themes of suicide and self-harm. Consequently, anyone under the age of 18 must be accompanied by a parent or guardian to attend the performances. This stipulation may affect families considering attending the show, as arrangements will need to be made for younger audience members.

Tickets are available for purchase through the theatre's website, offering various seating options, including standard theatre seating and immersive bench seating for a more engaging experience. Given the high demand, interested audience members are advised to secure their tickets promptly to avoid disappointment. The return of this production not only highlights the cultural vibrancy of Ramsbottom but also underscores the community's appetite for thought-provoking theatre, which may encourage further artistic endeavours in the area.

title: Heywood couple uses football to strengthen bonds with foster children
excerpt: Lauren and Danny Sullivan have harnessed their passion for football to create a nurturing environment for young people in their care, fostering connections and easing transitions into family life since 2012.
content_html:
Lauren and Danny Sullivan, a fostering couple from Heywood, have underscored the significant role that football plays in forging strong connections with the young people they care for. Since embarking on their fostering journey with Rochdale Borough Council in 2012, the couple has welcomed five boys, aged between 12 and 21, into their home, alongside their nine-year-old son, Daniel. Football has become a cornerstone of their daily routine, with the family actively participating in coaching, training sessions, and weekend matches.

The Sullivans have expressed their delight in witnessing the joy on the children's faces as they engage in play together in their garden after school or college. They have observed that football not only nurtures relationships but also aids children in adjusting to their new surroundings. A particularly memorable event was a Champions League final party held to welcome their first long-term foster child, which played a crucial role in easing his transition into the family.

Danny, who works as a football coach and scout, has taken an active interest in mentoring one of the 17-year-olds in their care, assisting him in obtaining coaching qualifications and becoming a grassroots coach. This mentorship has fostered a strong bond between them, facilitating discussions about coaching techniques and personal development. Another young person, who initially communicated solely in French, was able to integrate into the family swiftly through their shared love of football. Lauren noted that this common activity effectively broke down language barriers, enabling him to connect with the other boys almost immediately.

The couple hopes to inspire others to consider fostering, highlighting the transformative impact that love and support can have on young people within the care system. Their commitment to nurturing these relationships through football not only enriches the lives of the children but also contributes positively to the wider community.

Councillor Rachel Massey from Rochdale Borough Council commended the Sullivans for their dedication, emphasising the vital difference that foster carers make in providing stability and a sense of belonging for vulnerable children. As the need for foster families continues to grow, initiatives like those undertaken by the Sullivans may encourage more individuals to consider this rewarding path, potentially leading to improved outcomes for children in care across the borough.
